title: A CS Professor on Why Slow Learning Wins in the AI Era | CU Boulder, Tom Yeh
summary: Tom Yeh advocates teaching AI fundamentals by hand at human speed rather than focusing on latest tools, arguing that mathematical foundations provide durable skills resilient to hype cycles. He warns against the 'AI-Native' trap, emphasizing that people change AI rather than vice versa.
writeup: Tom Diet, professor of computer science at the University of Colorado Boulder and founder of the AI by Hand initiative, argues that foundational mathematical fluency (epitomized by matrix multiplication, which underlies computer graphics, big data, machine learning, and now AI) is more durable than any specific tool or framework. His core claim: each technology wave replaces the surface layer while leaving the same mathematical substrate intact, so practitioners who internalize foundations can rebuild expertise on demand, while those who chase surface features must rebuild from nothing each cycle. The talk weaves together a Korean palace metaphor (the palace burned but its stone foundation survived to anchor a later rebuild), the pedagogical philosophy of handwritten derivation as a pacing and focus mechanism, and a critique of the educational incentive system that rewards credentialing over genuine comprehension.
key_points: Matrix multiplication has been the operative primitive across every major computing wave: computer graphics (CGI after Jurassic Park), big data processing, machine learning, and current AI, with quantum computing likely to continue the pattern, making it the clearest example of an evergreen foundation.

The AI by Hand method forces human-speed instruction: the instructor can only write as fast as a hand permits, students can only follow at that pace, and note-taking keeps hands off devices, solving a focus problem that slide-based and live-coding instruction cannot.

Diet draws an explicit analogy to Gyeongbokgung Palace in Seoul: destroyed by fire in the 1500s except for its stone foundation, then rebuilt in the 1800s on that same base. The lesson he draws is that rebuilding a skill on a solid foundation is fast; practitioners who ignore foundations must rebuild the entire structure every cycle.

The value of working through foundational AI math is not recalled equations but demonstrated willingness to open the black box and invest sustained effort. Diet argues that willingness to take on hard learning challenges is the differentiating trait employers actually screen for, and AI fluency follows automatically from hiring people who are genuine problem-solvers.

Chegg was put out of business by AI, yet academic dishonesty persists unchanged. Diet reads this as evidence that Chegg and AI are symptoms of a broken incentive system that does not reward real learning, and that fixing the upstream cause (defining what learning actually means) matters more than defeating any particular cheating mechanism.

Diet frames personal non-AI skills (piano, soccer, any discipline that required patient, effortful acquisition) as a transferable meta-skill: the capacity to acquire a difficult competency is identity-level and tool-agnostic, so skipping a piano practice is more costly long-term than skipping any single AI tool.
